title: Simple Playhouse: Front Wall with Door
summary: The front wall of the Simple Playhouse series — a 2x2-framed stud wall skinned in 1/4" plywood with a working arched-top door and a window, all furring-strip cheap.
description:
This is one part of the Simple Playhouse series (the deck, back wall, gable ends, and roof are their own plans), and it's the wall with the personality: the door and front window. It's built exactly like a real house wall at 2/3 scale — plates, studs at framing spacing, headers over the openings — except the 'studs' are 2x2 furring strips and the sheathing is 1/4" plywood, because the only load this wall carries is imagination and the occasional leaning kid.

The money-saving trick is in step 1, and it rewards a careful hand: trace the door and window openings onto the plywood, and cut the DOOR opening cleanly — because that cutout becomes the door itself. One sheet, zero waste, and a door that matches its opening's grain like it grew there. Score your cut lines and go slowly; a ragged cut here costs you the free door.

The framing math builds in the fun part: the arched door header gets cut to a curve, and the door is trimmed out in 1x2/1x3 with pocket holes. Trim is measure-as-you-go per the source — furring-strip walls wander a little, and trim cut to the wall you actually built always beats trim cut to the wall on paper.

Size note for parents: the door is kid-scale on purpose. Adults enter on their knees, which the kids consider a feature. Use exterior plywood and exterior fasteners — this lives outside — and hang the door on hinges sized for its featherweight (light gate or heavy cabinet hinges both work).

tools.note: Framing cuts and the plywood — cut the door opening cleanly; the cutout IS the door.
1-1/4" nails for sheathing and trim, 1" for the door skin.
The arched door header and the window opening corners.
If your nailer won't shoot 1" brads, hand-nail the door skin.
Every edge a kid can touch.

materials.name: Exterior plywood, 1/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Wall skin — the door comes out of the door opening cutout.

materials.name: 2x2 furring strip, 8 ft
materials.note: Wall framing. Cull the twisted ones ruthlessly.

materials.name: Hinges
materials.note: Light gate or heavy cabinet hinges — the door weighs almost nothing.

materials.name: Exterior paint
materials.note: The kids get a vote on color. You will lose the vote.

cutList.note: The bottom plate breaks at the door opening — no plate across a doorway.
Door and window cut out to match the framed openings — save the door cutout.
57-1/4" and 14-3/4" — breaks at the door like the plate.
Gets the arch. No 1x4 scrap? Use a 1x3 and lengthen the door sides to 34".

steps.title: Frame the wall and skin it
steps.body:
Frame the wall flat on the ground — plates, six studs, window box, door header — with 2" screws and glue, checking square by diagonals. Note the bottom plate breaks at the door opening; nobody trips over a threshold in a playhouse either.

Trace the window and door openings onto the plywood from the framed wall, and cut the DOOR opening carefully — that cutout is your door blank. Then glue and nail the skin to the framing with 1-1/4" brads.

steps.title: Trim the wall — cut as you go
steps.body:
Run the 1x3 top and bottom trim and 1x2 side, door, and window trim, keeping outside edges flush. Measure each piece against the wall you actually built rather than pre-cutting the list — furring-strip framing wanders a touch, and trim's whole job is covering that gracefully.

1-1/4" brads and glue throughout.

steps.title: Build the door from the cutout
steps.body: Cut the arch into the door's 1x4 header piece with the jigsaw, then pocket-screw the door frame together — sides, middle rail, bottom, arched header. Glue and nail the saved plywood cutout to the back of the frame with 1" brads (hand-nail if your gun won't go that short). Do not skip the glue; the skin is the door's rigidity.

steps.title: Hang the door, sand, paint
steps.body:
Hang the door on its hinges with an even gap, and check it swings free through the whole arc — kids will test it roughly a thousand times the first week.

Sand every edge and corner to kid-grade smooth, then paint. This wall joins the deck, back wall, gable ends, and roof from the rest of the Simple Playhouse series — build order: deck, walls, roof.

images.alt: Playhouse front wall with framed door opening and window, trimmed and painted
